fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility library for container image version tracking. Basically our platform team keeps running into issues where they can't reliably tell if a newer image is available — the tag formats are all over the place and every engineer is hand-rolling their own string parsing which keeps breaking in subtle ways (especially with those debian-style suffixed tags, you know the ones). We also keep getting burned by false positives when detecting which registry a host belongs to — someone's test environment had a domain like `foodocker.io` and it was being picked up as Docker Hub, which caused some fun incidents.

The library should handle parsing tags into something comparable, doing the older/newer check correctly (there's some tricky logic here similar to how we handled pre-release ordering in that semver validation work we did for the auth service — check how that was approached), splitting image paths per registry since each one has different conventions for single-segment paths, and routing a full image URL to the right registry handler.

We need this to be properly structured — not just one big file — with clean separation between the version logic and the registry-specific stuff. Each registry (Docker Hub, GCR, Quay) should live in its own unit. The whole thing needs to be testable via a JSON-in / line-based-out adapter but that adapter should stay decoupled from the core logic. Edge cases like empty strings and unknown registries must not crash anything.

trigger_keywords: how do I parse a tag that starts with a letter
what happens when tag has no leading number
non-numeric tag parsing behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a tag does not begin with a digit (after stripping an optional leading 'v'), the version is 0.0.0 and the entire original tag string — including the leading 'v' if present — is preserved verbatim as the metadata field. For example, 'hello-1.2.3' yields major=0, minor=0, patch=0, metadata=hello-1.2.3.

trigger_keywords: how are missing version components handled
what if the tag only has major and minor
default value for absent patch number
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any of the three numeric components (major, minor, patch) that are absent from the tag string default to 0. So '1.2' parses as major=1, minor=2, patch=0, metadata=empty. Only up to the first three dot-separated numeric groups are consumed as version numbers; the first non-numeric dot-group stops the numeric parsing and everything from that point onward becomes the metadata suffix.

trigger_keywords: pre-release tag ordering
should a release beat a pre-release at the same version
tag with no suffix vs tag with alpha suffix which is newer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A tag that has no metadata suffix is considered the newer (final) release compared to the same numeric version that carries a metadata suffix. Concretely, if first has no metadata and second does (same major/minor/patch), less_than is false — the clean release outranks the pre-release. The inverse is also true: a pre-release of a given version is older than the final release of that same version.

trigger_keywords: how metadata tokens are compared
numeric segment in suffix compared numerically
r9 vs r39 ordering in tag metadata
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the numeric major/minor/patch triple is equal and both tags have metadata, each metadata suffix is tokenised into alternating runs of digits and non-digits. Digit runs are compared as integers (so r9 < r39 because 9 < 39), and non-digit runs are compared lexicographically (so 'alpha' < 'beta'). Token-by-token comparison proceeds left to right; the side that exhausts its tokens first is considered older.

trigger_keywords: host matching substring false positive
foodocker.io should not match docker
how exact is the host matching logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Host matching is strict: a host matches a registry only if it is exactly equal to one of the registry's canonical domains, or if it is a proper subdomain ending in '.<canonical-domain>'. A string that merely contains the canonical domain as a substring — with characters glued to the left like 'foodocker.io', or to the right like 'docker.iofoo' — must NOT match. An empty host string never matches any registry.

trigger_keywords: docker hub canonical domains
does docker match both docker.io and docker.com
which base domains does docker registry accept
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Docker Hub is special in that it recognises two canonical base domains: docker.io and docker.com. A host matches if it equals exactly 'docker.io' or 'docker.com', or ends in '.docker.io' or '.docker.com'. GCR uses only 'gcr.io' and Quay uses only 'quay.io' as their single canonical base domains.

trigger_keywords: single segment path docker hub default repo
what is the default repository for bare image name on docker
nginx with no slash on docker hub
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For Docker Hub, a bare single-segment path (no slashes) is treated as an official library image: the repository becomes 'library' and the image name is the segment itself. When the path has two or more segments, only the last two are used — the second-to-last is the repository and the last is the image; any earlier segments are discarded.

trigger_keywords: gcr single segment default repo
what repo does gcr use for a bare image name
kube-scheduler with no slash on gcr
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For GCR, a bare single-segment path defaults to repository 'google-containers' with the segment as the image name. When the path contains slashes, everything before the final slash becomes the repository (all intermediate segments are preserved, unlike Docker Hub which discards them) and the final segment is the image.

trigger_keywords: quay single segment path behavior
bare path on quay with no slash
what happens to image field when quay path has one segment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For Quay, a bare single-segment path (no slashes) sets the repository to that segment and leaves the image field empty (empty string). When the path contains slashes, everything before the final slash is the repository and the final segment is the image, preserving all intermediate segments in the repository portion.

trigger_keywords: url routing fallback for unknown host
what registry handles a url with no recognisable host
bare image name with no slash in url routing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When routing a URL, the first slash-delimited segment is tested as a candidate host against Docker Hub, GCR, and Quay rules. If no slash exists, or the leading segment matches no known registry, the URL falls back to Docker Hub with host set to empty string and the entire original URL string used as the path. The registry field in that case is 'docker'.

trigger_keywords: empty tag comparison
how are two empty strings compared for version ordering
ordering rule when one or both tags are empty
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If either tag in a comparison is empty, ordering is determined purely by string length: the shorter string is 'less than' the longer. If both are the same length (including both empty), less_than is false. This rule takes priority over all other ordering logic.

trigger_keywords: code structure requirements
should all registries be in one file
how should the project be organised into modules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must be split into distinct units by responsibility: version parsing/comparison logic lives in its own module, and each registry (Docker Hub, GCR, Quay) lives in its own sub-unit under a registry/client package. The JSON stdin/stdout adapter is a separate execution layer that must not be mixed with core domain logic. The router must depend on a registry abstraction (interface), not on concrete registry implementations directly, so adding a new registry means adding a new unit rather than editing the router.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'parse_version', 'compare_versions', 'match_host', 'split_path', 'route_url'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled pre-release ordering in that semver validation work we did for the auth service
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The metadata suffix comparison logic described in feature2_version_comparison.json: tokenise each suffix into alternating digit/non-digit runs, compare digit runs numerically and non-digit runs lexicographically, left to right, with the shorter token list losing on exhaustion.

context_pointers.vague_reference: each one has different conventions for single-segment paths
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Docker Hub defaults to repo='library'; GCR defaults to repo='google-containers'; Quay sets repo=the segment itself and image=empty string — as specified in feature4_1, feature4_2, and feature4_3 path split test cases.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that particular aspect in my notes right now. Could you check the existing test cases in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases folder or look at how the related feature is described in the PRD? That should give you the authoritative behaviour — and if something still seems ambiguous after that, flag it and we can discuss.

feature1_version_parsing.json.description: Parse a container image tag into a three-part numeric version (major, minor, patch) plus a free-form metadata suffix. A leading 'v' is optional. Missing numeric components default to 0. Any text that follows the patch number (or any text that prevents a leading numeric match) is preserved verbatim as metadata. Tags that do not start with a number yield a zero version and carry the whole string as metadata.

feature2_version_comparison.json.description: Determine whether one image tag represents an older version than another, returning a boolean ordering result. Comparison first weighs the numeric major/minor/patch triple; ties are broken by tokenising the metadata suffix into alternating number/word segments and comparing them left-to-right (numbers compared numerically, words lexicographically). A tag with no metadata is considered newer than the same numeric version with a pre-release metadata suffix. Empty tags order purely by length. Two arbitrary non-numeric strings never order as less-than.

feature3_1_host_match_docker.json.description: Decide whether a registry host string belongs to the Docker Hub registry. A host matches only when it is exactly docker.io or docker.com, or any subdomain ending in .docker.io / .docker.com. Strings that merely contain the substring (prefix or suffix glued on) do not match, and an empty host never matches.

feature3_2_host_match_gcr.json.description: Decide whether a registry host string belongs to the GCR registry. A host matches only when it is exactly gcr.io or any subdomain ending in .gcr.io. Strings that merely contain the substring (prefix or suffix glued on) do not match, and an empty host never matches.

feature3_3_host_match_quay.json.description: Decide whether a registry host string belongs to the Quay registry. A host matches only when it is exactly quay.io or any subdomain ending in .quay.io. Strings that merely contain the substring (prefix or suffix glued on) do not match, and an empty host never matches.

feature4_1_path_split_docker.json.description: Split a Docker Hub image path into a repository and image name. A bare single segment is treated as an official image under the implicit 'library' repository. When the path has two or more segments, only the last two are used: the second-to-last is the repository and the last is the image (any leading registry segments are discarded).

feature4_2_path_split_gcr.json.description: Split a GCR image path into a repository and image name. A bare single segment is treated as an image under the implicit 'google-containers' repository. When the path contains slashes, everything before the last slash becomes the repository (preserving intermediate segments) and the final segment is the image.

feature4_3_path_split_quay.json.description: Split a Quay image path into a repository and image name. A bare single segment is treated as the repository with an empty image. When the path contains slashes, everything before the last slash becomes the repository (preserving intermediate segments) and the final segment is the image.

feature5_url_routing.json.description: Route a full image URL to the registry that should handle it, also reporting the host and the remaining path. The first slash-delimited segment is treated as a candidate host: if it matches the Docker, GCR, or Quay host rules the URL is routed there with that host and the remainder as path. A URL with no slash, or whose leading segment matches no known registry host, falls back to the Docker registry with an empty host and the entire original string as the path.
